title: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis

items.title: Understanding CDI: Common Causes and Risk Factors
items.content: Clostridioides difficile infection (CDI) is primarily associated with antimicrobial use, leading to disruption of normal colonic microbiota. It occurs frequently in healthcare settings due to high levels of antibiotic use and environmental contamination by C. difficile spores.
keyPoints: CDI is the most common hospital-acquired diarrheal illness.
Risk increases with prolonged hospital stays; colonization rates can be ≥20% for patients hospitalized >2 weeks.
Antibiotics like clindamycin, cephalosporins, and fluoroquinolones are frequently implicated in CDI outbreaks.
Environmental contamination by C. difficile spores is a significant factor in healthcare settings.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: CDI Epidemiology: Trends and Statistics
items.content: The burden of CDI has decreased by 24% from 2011 to 2017, primarily due to reductions in healthcare-associated cases. Community-associated CDI rates have remained stable but now approximate those seen in healthcare settings.
keyPoints: In 2017, there were an estimated 462,100 cases of CDI in the United States.
The total burden of CDI decreased by 24% from 2011 to 2017, mainly due to a reduction in healthcare-associated cases.
Community-associated CDI rates have remained unchanged but now approximate healthcare-associated rates.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: Pathogenesis of CDI: Toxins and Clinical Manifestations
items.content: Toxigenic C. difficile produces toxins A and B, which disrupt epithelial-cell barrier function leading to diarrhea and pseudomembranous colitis. Toxin B is considered the more critical virulence factor.
keyPoints: Toxin A (enterotoxin) and toxin B (cytotoxin) are produced by C. difficile.
Both toxins disrupt epithelial-cell barrier function, causing diarrhea and pseudomembrane formation.
Toxin B is a more significant virulence factor than toxin A, as clinical disease occurs with toxin A-negative but not toxin B-negative strains.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)

items.title: Why are colonized patients with nontoxigenic C. difficile strains at decreased risk for CDI?
items.content: Colonization with nontoxigenic strains of C. difficile may confer a protective effect against Clostridioides difficile infection (CDI). This is because these strains do not produce the toxins responsible for causing disease, thus reducing the likelihood of developing symptomatic CDI.
keyPoints: Patients colonized with nontoxigenic strains have decreased risk of CDI.
Nontoxigenic strains do not produce disease-causing toxins.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 2)

items.title: How does the host immune response influence susceptibility to recurrent CDI?
items.content: The development of serum antibodies to C. difficile toxins, particularly toxin B, plays a crucial role in protecting against recurrent CDI. Intravenous monoclonal antibodies targeting these toxins have been shown to significantly reduce recurrence rates when used alongside standard antibiotic therapy.
keyPoints: Serum antibodies to toxin A and B protect against recurrent CDI.
Monoclonal antibodies to toxin B alone significantly lower recurrence rates compared to placebo plus standard therapy.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 2)

items.title: Understanding the Impact and Characteristics of Epidemic C. difficile Strains
items.content: The NAP1/BI/027 strain significantly contributed to increased CDI rates due to its high toxin production, binary toxin presence, and fluoroquinolone resistance.
keyPoints: NAP1/BI/027 produces 16–23 times more toxins A and B than control strains in vitro.
Presence of binary toxin CDT in NAP1/BI/027.
High-level resistance to all fluoroquinolones in NAP1/BI/027.
Fluoroquinolone restriction correlated with a decline in CDI cases in England by 2013.
Decrease in health care-associated isolates of NAPI/BI/027 from 31% to 15% (2011-2017) in the U.S.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Recognizing Clinical Manifestations and Red Flags of C. difficile Infection
items.content: Diarrhea is a primary symptom of CDI, with additional findings such as fever, abdominal pain, leukocytosis, and potential adynamic ileus indicating severity.
keyPoints: Diarrhea in CDI ranges from soft to watery or mucoid without gross blood.
Fever present in 28% of cases, abdominal pain in 22%, and leukocytosis in 50%.
Adynamic ileus seen on x-ray in ~20% of cases can lead to overlooked CDI diagnosis.
Unexplained leukocytosis with ≥15,000 WBCs/μL suggests high risk for complications like toxic megacolon and sepsis.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Diagnostic Criteria for Clostridioides difficile Infection (CDI)
items.content: The diagnosis of CDI is established by combining clinical criteria: the presence of diarrhea, defined as ≥3 unformed stools per 24 hours for ≥2 days with no other recognized cause, and detection of toxin A or B in stool. Additional diagnostic methods include nucleic acid amplification testing (NAAT) like PCR, culture for toxin-producing C. difficile, or visualization of pseudomembranes during endoscopy.
keyPoints: Diarrhea criteria: ≥3 unformed stools per 24 hours for ≥2 days
Detection methods: Toxin A/B in stool, NAAT (PCR), culture, or pseudomembrane visualization
Endoscopy visualizes PMC in ~50% of positive stool cultures with toxin assay
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Challenges and Recommendations in CDI Testing Algorithms
items.content: No single test for C. difficile achieves high sensitivity, specificity, and rapid turnaround. NAATs are sensitive but may detect colonization rather than infection. European guidelines recommend confirming toxin presence alongside NAAT or GDH positivity; U.S. guidelines suggest including a stool toxin test when no prior criteria exist. Discrepant results (NAAT+ or GDH+/toxin EIA-) often lead to CDI treatment, and empirical treatment is advised if testing is delayed.
keyPoints: No single test has high sensitivity, specificity, and rapid turnaround
NAATs are sensitive but may detect colonization
European guidelines: Confirm toxin presence with NAAT/GDH positivity
U.S. guidelines: Include stool toxin test when no prior criteria exist
>50% of patients harbor organism post-treatment; tests not predictive of recurrence
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: First-Line Treatment for CDI: Fidaxomicin vs. Vancomycin
items.content: The 2021 IDSA and SHEA guidelines recommend fidaxomicin as the first-line treatment for Clostridioides difficile infection (CDI) in adults, with oral vancomycin as an acceptable alternative due to resource availability issues. Fidaxomicin has been shown to significantly reduce the rate of recurrent CDI compared to vancomycin.
keyPoints: Fidaxomicin is suggested as first-line treatment for CDI per 2021 IDSA/SHEA guidelines.
Oral vancomycin remains an acceptable alternative when fidaxomicin is not available.
Clinical trials show comparable resolution of diarrhea (~90%) with both treatments, but fidaxomicin has a significantly lower recurrence rate.
Vancomycin cure rate (81%) was superior to metronidazole (73%; p = .034) in the largest randomized trial.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · Clostridioides difficile Infection

items.title: Indications for Prompt CDI Treatment Initiation
items.content: Prompt initiation of specific treatment is crucial, especially with the NAP1/BI/027 epidemic strain. Patients who appear seriously ill should receive oral vancomycin or fidaxomicin immediately, particularly if they have a high WBC count (>15,000/μL) or elevated creatinine level (≥1.5 mg/dL).
keyPoints: Discontinuation of antimicrobials is the first step in CDI treatment; 15–23% respond to this alone.
Prompt specific treatment initiation is standard due to rapid clinical deterioration with NAP1/BI/027 strain.
Serious illness indicators: high WBC count (>15,000/μL) or creatinine level (≥1.5 mg/dL).
Treatment duration for vancomycin or fidaxomicin should be at least 10 days.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · Clostridioides difficile Infection

items.title: Why Fidaxomicin is Preferred for Reducing Recurrence Rates in CDI
items.content: Fidaxomicin demonstrates a superior efficacy over vancomycin in reducing recurrence rates of Clostridioides difficile infection (CDI). Patients treated with fidaxomicin have significantly lower recurrence rates compared to those treated with vancomycin. This is particularly important for patients experiencing their first CDI recurrence, as they are at an even higher risk for subsequent recurrences.
keyPoints: 15–30% of successfully treated patients experience recurrences of CDI following treatment.
Fidaxomicin reduces further recurrences more effectively than vancomycin in patients with one CDI recurrence.
Recurrent disease poses an 11% risk of serious complications such as shock, megacolon, perforation, colectomy, or death within 30 days.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · RECURRENT CDI

items.title: How to Manage Multiple Recurrences and High-Risk Patients for rCDI
items.content: For patients with multiple recurrences of CDI, treatment options include vancomycin in a tapering/pulsed regimen, fidaxomicin in standard or extended dosing, vancomycin followed by rifaximin, or fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT). FMT has become more practical with FDA-approved microbiota replacement therapies. Bezlotoxumab, an adjunctive treatment for high-risk patients receiving standard antibacterial agents, reduces the risk of recurrent CDI by ~10%.
keyPoints: No standard treatment exists for multiple recurrences; vancomycin tapering/pulsed regimen is a practical approach.
FMT via various methods and FDA-approved microbiota replacement therapies are options for preventing further recurrence.
Bezlotoxumab reduces the risk of rCDI by ~10% in high-risk patients receiving standard antibacterial agents.
Risk factors for rCDI include age >65 years, immunocompromise, severe CDI on presentation, and prior episode within 6 months.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · RECURRENT CDI

items.title: Recognizing Fulminant CDI in Patients Without Diarrhea
items.content: Fulminant Clostridioides difficile infection (CDI) can present without diarrhea, mimicking an acute surgical abdomen. Key diagnostic considerations include the presence of sepsis and signs of toxic megacolon or obstruction on imaging.
keyPoints: Sepsis may manifest as hypotension, fever, tachycardia, leukocytosis (≥20,000 WBCs/μL).
Diagnosis should consider CDI in patients with recent antibiotic use if presenting with acute abdomen or sepsis.
Diagnostic tests: cautious sigmoidoscopy or colonoscopy for PMC visualization and abdominal CT.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · SEVERE COMPLICATED OR FULMINANT CDI

items.title: Management Strategies for Fulminant CDI
items.content: Medical management of fulminant CDI is challenging due to ileus, which hinders oral medication delivery. A combination of vancomycin and IV metronidazole or IV tigecycline may be used, with surgical colectomy as a potential life-saving intervention.
keyPoints: Oral fidaxomicin, metronidazole, or vancomycin are difficult to deliver due to ileus.
Vancomycin (oral/nasogastric tube/retention enema) plus IV metronidazole has shown some success.
IV tigecycline used in small-scale studies; surgical colectomy may be necessary if medical management fails.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · SEVERE COMPLICATED OR FULMINANT CDI

items.question: Which antibiotic class is most recently implicated in hospital outbreaks of Clostridioides difficile infection?
items.explanation: The fluoroquinolones, including ciprofloxacin, levofloxacin, and moxifloxacin, are the most recent drug class implicated in hospital outbreaks of CDI.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)

items.question: What is a common risk factor for Clostridioides difficile infection related to patient care?
items.explanation: The use of electronic rectal thermometers is identified as a risk factor for CDI.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)

items.question: What percentage of community residents are typically colonized by C. difficile?
items.explanation: The rate of fecal colonization among community residents is 1–3%.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)

items.question: Which toxin is considered the more important virulence factor in Clostridioides difficile infection?
items.explanation: Toxin B may be the more important virulence factor, as clinical disease occurs with toxin A-negative strains but not with toxin B-negative strains.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT proposed as a third essential event for the development of CDI?
options: Exposure to a C. difficile strain of particular virulence
Exposure to antimicrobial agents especially likely to cause CDI
items.explanation: Colonization by nontoxigenic strains is associated with a decreased risk of CDI, not an essential event for its development.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 2)

items.question: What is the role of serum IgG antibody response to toxin A in CDI?
options: It determines which patients develop diarrhea
It has no effect on CDI development
items.explanation: The anamnestic immune response, including serum IgG antibody response to toxin A, is a factor in determining which patients develop diarrhea.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 2)

items.question: Which statement about the development of antibodies to C. difficile toxins is correct?
options: Most humans first develop antibodies during adulthood
Infants typically develop symptomatic CDI due to high antibody levels
Antibodies to both toxin A and B protect against recurrent CDI in adults
Antibody to toxin A alone effectively prevents recurrent CDI
items.explanation: In adulthood, serum antibodies to both toxin A and B are protective against recurrent CDI.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 2)

items.question: Which characteristic is NOT associated with the epidemic strain NAP1/BI/027 of C. difficile?
options: Ability to produce significantly more toxin A and B than control strains in vitro.
Grossly bloody stools as a common manifestation.
items.explanation: The passage states that diarrhea caused by C. difficile is almost never grossly bloody, which is not a characteristic of the NAP1/BI/027 strain.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: What was one of the outcomes in England after instituting national control policies for CDI in 2006?
options: Near elimination of fluoroquinolone-resistant strains of C. difficile by 2013.
Introduction of a new epidemic strain.
Decrease in the production of toxins A and B.
items.explanation: The passage mentions that restriction of fluoroquinolones correlated with near elimination of fluoroquinolone-resistant strains by 2013.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: What is a common clinical manifestation of C. difficile infection?
options: Toxic megacolon as an initial symptom.
items.explanation: The passage states that fever occurs in 28% of CDI cases, making it a common clinical manifestation.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: Which combination is required for the diagnosis of Clostridioides difficile infection (CDI)?
options: Diarrhea with no other recognized cause and detection of toxin A or B in the stool.
Detection of pseudomembranes in the colon without diarrhea.
Positive NAAT result alone, regardless of clinical symptoms.
Empirical treatment based on delayed stool testing.
items.explanation: Diagnosis requires both diarrhea with no other recognized cause and detection of toxin A or B in the stool.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What is a limitation of using PCR for diagnosing CDI?
options: PCR may detect colonization with toxigenic C. difficile even if diarrhea is due to other causes.
PCR results are always conclusive without further testing.
PCR cannot be used in conjunction with toxin assays.
items.explanation: Concerns exist that PCR may detect colonization with toxigenic C. difficile in patients who have diarrhea for reasons other than CDI.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Why are tests of cure following treatment not recommended for CDI?
options: More than 50% of patients continue to harbor the organism and its toxin after diarrhea has ceased.
They have high sensitivity but low specificity.
Such tests can accurately predict recurrence of CDI.
items.explanation: Tests of cure are not recommended because more than 50% of patients continue to harbor the organism and its toxin after diarrhea has ceased, and results do not predict recurrence.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which of the following is recommended as first-line treatment for Clostridioides difficile infection (CDI) according to the 2021 IDSA and SHEA guidelines?
items.explanation: Fidaxomicin is recommended as first-line treatment for CDI in the 2021 IDSA and SHEA guidelines.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · Clostridioides difficile Infection

items.question: Which of the following statements about metronidazole use in CDI management is correct?
options: Metronidazole is recommended as first-line treatment for severe CDI.
IV Vancomycin is preferred over oral vancomycin for mild to moderate CDI.
Oral Metronidazole is only recommended when fidaxomicin or vancomycin are not available.
Metronidazole has been approved by the FDA specifically for CDI treatment.
items.explanation: Oral metronidazole is recommended only for mild or moderate CDI when fidaxomicin or vancomycin is not available.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · Clostridioides difficile Infection

items.question: What is the efficacy of IV Vancomycin in treating Clostridioides difficile infection?
options: It is highly effective for CDI.
It should be used as a first-line treatment.
It is recommended only when oral administration is not possible.
items.explanation: IV Vancomycin is ineffective for CDI.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · Clostridioides difficile Infection

items.question: Which antibiotic is associated with a lower recurrence rate of Clostridioides difficile infection (CDI) compared to vancomycin?
items.explanation: Fidaxomicin is associated with a lower recurrence rate of CDI than vancomycin.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · RECURRENT CDI

items.question: Which treatment option is not recommended for recurrent Clostridioides difficile infection (CDI)?
items.explanation: Metronidazole is not recommended for the treatment of recurrent CDI.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · RECURRENT CDI

items.question: What adjunctive treatment has been shown to reduce the risk of recurrent Clostridioides difficile infection (rCDI) by an absolute rate of ~10%?
items.explanation: Bezlotoxumab reduces the risk of rCDI by an absolute rate of ~10% when administered with standard-of-care antibacterial agents.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · RECURRENT CDI

items.question: Which of the following is a typical presentation in patients with fulminant Clostridioides difficile infection (CDI)?
options: Sepsis characterized by hypotension, fever, tachycardia, and leukocytosis
Rapid resolution of symptoms with oral antibiotics alone
items.explanation: Fulminant CDI can present with sepsis including hypotension, fever, tachycardia, and leukocytosis.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · SEVERE COMPLICATED OR FULMINANT CDI

items.question: What is the best diagnostic approach for fulminant CDI in patients without diarrhea?
options: Empirical antibiotic therapy based on clinical suspicion alone
Immediate surgical intervention without further diagnostics
Cautious sigmoidoscopy or colonoscopy and abdominal CT examination
Routine blood tests including complete blood count
items.explanation: In patients without diarrhea, cautious sigmoidoscopy or colonoscopy to visualize PMC and an abdominal CT are the best diagnostic tests.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · SEVERE COMPLICATED OR FULMINANT CDI

items.question: Which combination of treatments has been used with some success in uncontrolled studies for fulminant CDI?
options: Vancomycin (orally or via nasogastric tube) plus IV metronidazole
items.explanation: The combination of vancomycin and IV metronidazole has been used with some success in uncontrolled studies.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · SEVERE COMPLICATED OR FULMINANT CDI

items.statement: Clostridioides difficile spores are commonly found in the environment of hospitals and chronic-care facilities.
items.explanation: C. difficile spores are widely found in hospital environments.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)

items.statement: The pseudomembranes of PMC involve the entire colon wall in all patients.
items.explanation: While the whole colon is usually involved, about 10% of patients have rectal sparing.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · DEFINITION | ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 1)

items.statement: Patients colonized with C. difficile have a higher risk of developing CDI.
items.explanation: Colonized patients who have not previously had CDI actually have a decreased risk, possibly due to colonization by nontoxigenic strains.

items.statement: Antibody to toxin A alone is effective in preventing recurrent CDI.
items.explanation: Antibody to toxin A alone was found ineffective, whereas antibody to toxin B and the combination were effective.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY (part 2)

items.statement: The epidemic strain NAP1/BI/027 is characterized by the presence of binary toxin CDT.
items.explanation: The passage confirms that the NAP1/BI/027 strain has the characteristic of possessing binary toxin CDT.

items.statement: Grossly bloody stools are a common manifestation in patients with C. difficile infection.
items.explanation: The passage states that stools caused by C. difficile are almost never grossly bloody.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.statement: Endoscopy can rule out CDI if pseudomembranes are not visualized in patients with a positive stool culture.
items.explanation: A negative endoscopy result does not rule out CDI, even if pseudomembranes are not visualized.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Testing of asymptomatic patients is recommended for diagnosing CDI.
items.explanation: Testing of asymptomatic patients is not recommended except for epidemiologic study purposes.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Fidaxomicin has been shown to have a lower rate of recurrent CDI compared to vancomycin.
items.explanation: Two large clinical trials indicated that the rate of recurrent CDI was significantly lower with fidaxomicin.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · Clostridioides difficile Infection

items.statement: Metronidazole is approved by the FDA for treatment of Clostridioides difficile infection.
items.explanation: Metronidazole was never approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for CDI.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · Clostridioides difficile Infection

items.statement: Metronidazole is recommended for treating recurrent Clostridioides difficile infection (CDI).
items.explanation: Metronidazole is not recommended for the treatment of recurrent CDI.
items.reference: Clostridioides difficile Infection Including Pseudomembranous Colitis · RECURRENT CDI

items.statement: Fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) has been approved by the FDA as a treatment option for recurrent Clostridioides difficile infection.
items.explanation: The FDA has approved two microbiota replacement therapies, including fecal microbiota suspension and spores for oral delivery.

items.statement: Surgical colectomy is considered only after medical management fails to show any response.

items.statement: Fulminant CDI typically presents with diarrhea as a primary symptom.
items.explanation: Patients with fulminant disease often do not have diarrhea.

items.assertion: Toxin A and toxin B both contribute to the pathogenesis of Clostridioides difficile infection.
items.reason: Both toxins glucosylate GTP-binding proteins, disrupting epithelial-cell barrier function.
items.explanation: Both assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.reason: Fidaxomicin has a significantly lower rate of recurrent CDI compared to vancomycin.
